title: Vim in Other Editors
subtitle: VS Code, JetBrains, browsers — you can take it with you.
summary: Most popular editors and IDEs have a Vim emulation mode or plugin. Quality varies but the basics work everywhere. Browsers can have Vim-style keybindings via Vimium / Tridactyl.

blocks.text: Once Vim becomes muscle memory, you'll want it everywhere. Most major editors and many other tools support Vim emulation.

rows: Evil mode (the gold standard for non-Vim editors)

blocks.title: What Usually Works (and What Doesn't)
blocks.text:
*Always works:* modal editing, basic motions ({key:h}/{key:j}/{key:k}/{key:l}, {key:w}/{key:b}, {key:f}/{key:t}), basic operators ({key:d}/{key:c}/{key:y}), text objects ({key:i}{key:w}, {key:i}{key:"}), visual modes, search ({key:/}), basic Ex commands (`:s`, `:w`, `:q`).

*Often works:* macros, registers (often only the unnamed and a few special), marks, jumps.

*Rarely works perfectly:* the full Ex command set, advanced regexes, custom Vimscript, plugin ecosystem.

The *highest-fidelity* options are *real Vim/Neovim embedded as a backend* (vscode-neovim) or *very thorough re-implementations* (IdeaVim). Lighter emulations cover the basics but you'll miss the 10% of tricks you've gotten used to.
